items.myth: SVM always uses every training example at prediction time like k-NN.
items.reality: Only support vectors — training points with non-zero dual coefficients — contribute to the decision function. Other points can be removed after training without changing predictions.
items.example: LinearSVC.support_ might list 5% of indices on a clean dataset; the other 95% are never referenced in f(x).

items.myth: Large C always gives a better model.
items.reality: Large C narrows the margin and penalizes slack heavily, fitting training noise. Small C allows a wider margin with more tolerated violations — often better generalization on noisy data.
items.example: On moons data with label noise, C=100 may overfit outliers while C=0.1 achieves higher test accuracy.

items.myth: SVM and logistic regression are unrelated because one uses margins and one uses probabilities.
items.reality: Both learn linear separators wᵀx + b = 0 in input space. They differ in loss (hinge slack vs BCE) and output interpretation, not in the linear boundary family for kernel=linear.
items.example: On the same scaled 2D data, both can produce similar-looking lines but different score calibrations.

items.myth: Support vectors are only points exactly on the margin boundary.
items.reality: In soft-margin SVM, points inside the margin or on the wrong side can also be support vectors when their slack ξᵢ is active (non-zero αᵢ).
items.example: A mislabeled point inside the margin often appears in support_ even though y(wᵀx + b) < 1.
